vector_only.question: A financial bank is designing its core transaction system. The system requires that account balances on all nodes must remain consistent at all times. Even if a network partition occurs causing some nodes to disconnect, it would rather suspend service than allow reading old data. According to the CAP theorem, which two attributes did this system choose? And which one did it sacrifice?
vector_only.ground_truth: The system chose **Consistency** and **Partition Tolerance**, sacrificing **Availability**. This aligns with the characteristics of a CP system (such as the behavior of traditional RDBMS in distributed settings).
vector_only.answer:
根據上下文，該系統選擇了以下兩個屬性：

*   **一致性 (Consistency)**：因為它要求所有節點上的帳戶餘額必須始終保持一致，並且不允許讀取舊數據。上下文提到「所有節點應同時看到相同的數據」。
*   **分區容錯性 (Partition tolerance)**：因為它明確提到了「即使網絡分區發生導致某些節點斷開連接」的情況，這意味著系統必須能夠應對分區。上下文指出「系統即使在節點故障的情況下也能繼續運行」。

該系統犧牲了：

*   **可用性 (Availability)**：因為在網絡分區發生時，它寧願「暫停服務」也不允許讀取舊數據，這意味著系統在某些情況下無法響應請求。上下文定義可用性為「系統應始終滿足請求」。

這符合CAP定理的原則：「三者中只能保證其中兩者」。在這個場景中，為了在分區發生時保持一致性，系統犧牲了可用性。

hybrid.question: A financial bank is designing its core transaction system. The system requires that account balances on all nodes must remain consistent at all times. Even if a network partition occurs causing some nodes to disconnect, it would rather suspend service than allow reading old data. According to the CAP theorem, which two attributes did this system choose? And which one did it sacrifice?
hybrid.ground_truth: The system chose **Consistency** and **Partition Tolerance**, sacrificing **Availability**. This aligns with the characteristics of a CP system (such as the behavior of traditional RDBMS in distributed settings).
hybrid.answer:
根據上下文，這個金融銀行系統選擇了以下兩個屬性：

1.  **一致性 (Consistency)**：系統要求所有節點上的帳戶餘額必須始終保持一致。
2.  **分区容忍性 (Partition Tolerance)**：即使發生網路分區，系統也能處理這種情況（儘管它選擇暫停服務）。

而它犧牲了 **可用性 (Availability)**，因為在網路分區發生時，它寧願暫停服務也不允許讀取舊數據，這意味著系統無法始終響應請求。

fulltext_only.question: A financial bank is designing its core transaction system. The system requires that account balances on all nodes must remain consistent at all times. Even if a network partition occurs causing some nodes to disconnect, it would rather suspend service than allow reading old data. According to the CAP theorem, which two attributes did this system choose? And which one did it sacrifice?
fulltext_only.ground_truth: The system chose **Consistency** and **Partition Tolerance**, sacrificing **Availability**. This aligns with the characteristics of a CP system (such as the behavior of traditional RDBMS in distributed settings).
fulltext_only.answer:
根据上下文和系统需求：

该系统选择了**一致性 (Consistency)** 和 **分区容错性 (Partition tolerance)**。

它牺牲了**可用性 (Availability)**。

**解释：**
*   系统要求“所有节点上的账户余额必须始终保持一致”，这明确选择了**一致性 (Consistency)**。
*   系统考虑了“即使发生网络分区导致部分节点断开连接”的情况，这表明它需要应对**分区容错性 (Partition tolerance)**。
*   在发生网络分区时，系统选择“宁愿暂停服务也不允许读取旧数据”，这意味着它为了保证一致性，牺牲了**可用性 (Availability)**。

metrics.user_input: A financial bank is designing its core transaction system. The system requires that account balances on all nodes must remain consistent at all times. Even if a network partition occurs causing some nodes to disconnect, it would rather suspend service than allow reading old data. According to the CAP theorem, which two attributes did this system choose? And which one did it sacrifice?
retrieved_contexts:
CAP Theorem
•Proposed in 2000
•Consistency
•In a distributed database, consistency takes a bigger role
•All nodes should see the same data at the same time, which 
means that the replicas should be immediately updated . 
•Strong Consistency in this theorem. 
•Availability
•A request should always  be fulfilled by the system
•This is a paramount requirement of all web -centric organizations
•Partition tolerance
•The system continues to operate even in the event of a node 
failure
•The system will fail only if all nodes fail
•Only TWO of the THREE can be guaranteed
•Typically, P is always required because we don’t want our system to 
be unavailable.
•One node failure is very common.
•Simplified question: Choose C or A.
•Depends on the application scenarios.

CAP Theorem
•Consistency or Availability?
•Traditional databases prefer consistency .
•ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation and Durability.
•RDBMS is distributed, but conservative.
•2PC (2 -Phase Commit protocol): if one operation fails on at least one 
machine, all changes made at the other machines participating in the 
operation will be undone to maintain a consistent database state

CAP Theorem
•Consistency or Availability?
•Modern  databases prefer availability, which sacrifices consistency .
•BASE: a relaxed  ACID on consistency. 
•Basically Availability: The database appears to work most of the time.
•Soft-state: Data can have transient or temporary states that may 
change over time, even without external triggers or inputs. It 
describes the record’s transitional state when several applications 
update it simultaneously. 
•Eventually Consistency: The record will achieve consistency when all 
the concurrent updates have been completed. Although the replicas 
may be different, they will be identical eventually.

Consistency
•Any given transaction transforms a consistent state of the database into 
another consistent state, without necessarily preserving consistency at all 
intermediate points
•A database state is consistent if and only if it satisfies all defined 
constraints. 
•For example, transfer $100 from A to B.
•Two sub -steps
•Deduct $100 from A.
•Add $100 to B.
•Consistency constraints: the sum of balances should be equal 
after this transaction.
•At the point between Sub -step 1 and Sub -step 2, the sum is not 
equal.
•Nevertheless, the state after this transactions satisfies the 
constraints.
•Consistency is to ensure integrity
•Consistency types
•Strong consistency
•Eventual consistency
•Quorum consistency

Strong consistency
•Read will always  reflect the most recent write
•Make sure that the write to all replicas are completed before committing
•Latency: Need to wait for all the replica to respond
•Slower “write”
•Commit: The modification can be aware by the new operations.

CAP Theorem
•Consistency or Availability?
•NewSQL: a class of relational database management systems that seek to 
provide the scalability of NoSQL systems for online transaction 
processing (OLTP) workloads while maintaining the ACID guarantees of a 
traditional database system
•A new idea

Outlines
•Database Availability
•Database Consistency
•CAP Theorem
•Case Study: MongoDB

Eventual Consistency
•Eventual consistency is a property of a system where different nodes may 
have different versions of the data , but where state changes eventually  
propagate to all of the servers

Quorum Consistency 
•The majority of the replicas agree on the result
•Write: the majority of the servers need to confirm that they have 
persisted your change
•Read: the majority of the replicas need to respond so that the most 
up-to-date copy of the data can be found 
•Trade latency for consistency in eventually consistent stores
•Wait longer for the majority of the servers to respond, but you get 
the freshest data

Push Replication
•After a data update, the originating Data Processing (DP) node sends the 
changes to the replica nodes to ensure that data is immediately updated
•This type of replication focuses on maintaining data consistency
•it decreases data availability due to the latency involved in ensuring 
data consistency at all nodes

*   **一致性 (Consistency)**：因為它要求所有節點上的帳戶餘額必須始終保持一致，並且不允許讀取舊數據。上下文提到「所有節點應同時看到相同的數據」。
*   **分區容錯性 (Partition tolerance)**：因為它明確提到了「即使網絡分區發生導致某些節點斷開連接」的情況，這意味著系統必須能夠應對分區。上下文指出「系統即使在節點故障的情況下也能繼續運行」。

該系統犧牲了：

*   **可用性 (Availability)**：因為在網絡分區發生時，它寧願「暫停服務」也不允許讀取舊數據，這意味著系統在某些情況下無法響應請求。上下文定義可用性為「系統應始終滿足請求」。

這符合CAP定理的原則：「三者中只能保證其中兩者」。在這個場景中，為了在分區發生時保持一致性，系統犧牲了可用性。

CAP Theorem
•Proposed in 2000
•Consistency
•In a distributed database, consistency takes a bigger role
•All nodes should see the same data at the same time, which 
means that the replicas should be immediately updated . 
•Strong Consistency in this theorem. 
•Availability
•A request should always  be fulfilled by the system
•This is a paramount requirement of all web -centric organizations
•Partition tolerance
•The system continues to operate even in the event of a node 
failure
•The system will fail only if all nodes fail
•Only TWO of the THREE can be guaranteed
•Typically, P is always required because we don’t want our system to 
be unavailable.
•One node failure is very common.
•Simplified question: Choose C or A.
•Depends on the application scenarios.

Consistency
•Any given transaction transforms a consistent state of the database into 
another consistent state, without necessarily preserving consistency at all 
intermediate points
•A database state is consistent if and only if it satisfies all defined 
constraints. 
•For example, transfer $100 from A to B.
•Two sub -steps
•Deduct $100 from A.
•Add $100 to B.
•Consistency constraints: the sum of balances should be equal 
after this transaction.
•At the point between Sub -step 1 and Sub -step 2, the sum is not 
equal.
•Nevertheless, the state after this transactions satisfies the 
constraints.
•Consistency is to ensure integrity
•Consistency types
•Strong consistency
•Eventual consistency
•Quorum consistency

CAP Theorem
•Consistency or Availability?
•Traditional databases prefer consistency .
•ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation and Durability.
•RDBMS is distributed, but conservative.
•2PC (2 -Phase Commit protocol): if one operation fails on at least one 
machine, all changes made at the other machines participating in the 
operation will be undone to maintain a consistent database state

Network Partition 
(due to network failure)
•A network partition may segregate a primary into a partition with a 
minority of nodes
•When the primary detects that it can only see a minority of nodes in the 
replica set, the primary steps down as primary and becomes a secondary
•A member in the partition that can communicate with a majority of the 
voting members (including itself) holds an election to become the new 
primary

Eventual Consistency
•Eventual consistency is a property of a system where different nodes may 
have different versions of the data , but where state changes eventually  
propagate to all of the servers

Push Replication
•After a data update, the originating Data Processing (DP) node sends the 
changes to the replica nodes to ensure that data is immediately updated
•This type of replication focuses on maintaining data consistency
•it decreases data availability due to the latency involved in ensuring 
data consistency at all nodes

CAP Theorem
•Consistency or Availability?
•NewSQL: a class of relational database management systems that seek to 
provide the scalability of NoSQL systems for online transaction 
processing (OLTP) workloads while maintaining the ACID guarantees of a 
traditional database system
•A new idea

CAP Theorem
•Consistency or Availability?
•Modern  databases prefer availability, which sacrifices consistency .
•BASE: a relaxed  ACID on consistency. 
•Basically Availability: The database appears to work most of the time.
•Soft-state: Data can have transient or temporary states that may 
change over time, even without external triggers or inputs. It 
describes the record’s transitional state when several applications 
update it simultaneously. 
•Eventually Consistency: The record will achieve consistency when all 
the concurrent updates have been completed. Although the replicas 
may be different, they will be identical eventually.

Replica Overhead
•If the database is fragmented, the Distributed DBMS must decompose a 
query into subqueries to access the appropriate fragments
•If the database is replicated, the DDBMS must decide which copy to 
access
•A READ operation selects the nearest copy to satisfy the transaction
•A WRITE operation requires that all copies be selected and updated 
to satisfy the mutual consistency rule
•Steps:
•The TP sends a data request to each selected DP for execution
•The DP receives and executes each request and sends the data back 
to the TP
•The TP assembles the DP responses

Fragmentation: Pros andCons?
•Advantages
•Partitions could be stored on different storage devices, taking 
advantage of resources in parallel
•Bulk data operations: dropping a partition is quicker, since the data is 
already separated
•Efficient locking: DBMS may be able to lock only one partition when 
performing operations that require a read lock on the table, leaving 
other partitions available
•Better resilience in case of corruption: if only a partition is corrupted, 
other partitions remain functional, and recovery from backups is 
faster
•Partitioning makes it possible to store more data in one table than 
can be held on a single disk or file system partition
•Some queries can be greatly optimized in virtue of the fact that data 
satisfying a given WHERE clause can be stored only on one or more 
partitions, which automatically excludes any remaining partitions 
from the search

而它犧牲了 **可用性 (Availability)**，因為在網路分區發生時，它寧願暫停服務也不允許讀取舊數據，這意味著系統無法始終響應請求。

•Depends on the application scenarios.

another consistent state, without necessarily preserving consistency at all 
intermediate points
•A database state is consistent if and only if it satisfies all defined 
constraints. 
•For example, transfer $100 from A to B.
•Two sub -steps
•Deduct $100 from A.
•Add $100 to B.
•Consistency constraints: the sum of balances should be equal 
after this transaction.
•At the point between Sub -step 1 and Sub -step 2, the sum is not

•Nevertheless, the state after this transactions satisfies the

•Consistency types
•Strong consistency
•Eventual consistency

Fragmentation: Pros andCons?
•Advantages
•Partitions could be stored on different storage devices, taking 
advantage of resources in parallel
•Bulk data operations: dropping a partition is quicker, since the data is 
already separated
•Efficient locking: DBMS may be able to lock only one partition when

•Better resilience in case of corruption: if only a partition is corrupted, 
other partitions remain functional, and recovery from backups is 
faster
•Partitioning makes it possible to store more data in one table than 
can be held on a single disk or file system partition
•Some queries can be greatly optimized in virtue of the fact that data 
satisfying a given WHERE clause can be stored only on one or more 
partitions, which automatically excludes any remaining partitions 
from the search

Principals and Subjects
•Usually
•each subject is associated with a unique principal
•all subjects of a principal have identical rights (equal to the rights of the 
invoking principal)
•This case can be modeled by a one -to-one mapping between subjects and 
principals
•For simplicity, a principal and subject can be treated as identical concepts . 
•We use the principal itself to represent any subjects with this principal
•e.g., Alice  -> all subjects which are on behalf of Alice, such as self -service 
machine with Alice’s account, online library system with Alice’s account.

BitLocker
•Rather than encrypting individual files and directories, one by one, as in 
EFS
•BitLocker encrypts an entire volume
•When your PC boots, the Windows boot loader loads from the 
System Reserved partition, and the boot loader prompts for a 
password
•BitLocker then decrypts the drive and loads Windows
•The encryption is transparent
•Your files appear like they normally would on an unencrypted 
system, but they are stored on the disk in an encrypted form
•Preferably to have Trusted Platform Module (TPM) on your PC’s 
motherboard
•This chip generates and store the encryption keys that BitLocker 
uses

Network Partition 
(due to network failure)
•A network partition may segregate a primary into a partition with a 
minority of nodes
•When the primary detects that it can only see a minority of nodes in the 
replica set, the primary steps down as primary and becomes a secondary
•A member in the partition that can communicate with a majority of the 
voting members (including itself) holds an election to become the new 
primary

CAP Theorem
•Consistency or Availability?
•Traditional databases prefer consistency .
•ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation and Durability.

•2PC (2 -Phase Commit protocol): if one operation fails on at least one 
machine, all changes made at the other machines participating in the 
operation will be undone to maintain a consistent database state

Push Replication
•After a data update, the originating Data Processing (DP) node sends the 
changes to the replica nodes to ensure that data is immediately updated
•This type of replication focuses on maintaining data consistency
•it decreases data availability due to the latency involved in ensuring 
data consistency at all nodes

Principle of Least Privilege
•Grant specific object privileges to users (e.g., based on their current job 
duties)
•rather than broad system privileges that allow access to all objects in 
the database
•Revoke the privilege when job duties change/users leave the company
•Each User & Task should only have as much capability as it needs

Eventual Consistency
•Eventual consistency is a property of a system where different nodes may 
have different versions of the data , but where state changes eventually  
propagate to all of the servers
